item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goodness, what an amazing SCP. Absolutely enthralling from start to finish, thoroughly suffused with magic and mystery, and excellently paced for the duration. Very glad you decided to leave the story unresolved, too, as it preserves all the wonderful elements that make this special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup.

The use of the object pronoun signifies that the Foundation thinks that Aria could be an anomaly not to be humanized — even if evidence says that she might not be directly controlling the main anomaly — whereas the human pronouns for the other children reflect a view of them as victims and as people deserving of some basic respect for fellow man. Because Aria is unreachable and because the Foundation's few interactions with her have always been controlled by her, the Foundation can't be sure that she's as blameless as the others, especially when she has demonstrated some measure of influence over the tree in how she captured the exploration drone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed that all the instances of SCP-3082-1 are referred to using gendered pronouns (he, she, etc), while SCP-3082-2 is referred to as "it". Is this intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. Not least because I find it a rather interesting change of pace to have the Foundation be (broadly) benevolent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this entire article was an excuse to use a polar coordinate system to navigate around a tree, wasn't it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All fair points. I'll keep this in mind in future writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My rationale for no-voting is somewhat similar to Shaggy's. Put simply, there are two competing storylines and it takes you 2/3 of the article to get to the second one; what's more, the connection between these two storylines is kinda tenuous. Personally, I think the idea of a reality bender turning into a giant fucking tree is cool, but not much in the article happens as a consequence of that—that is, Jacquelyn being the tree and the reason this pocket dimension exists doesn't do much except answer why everything is the way it is; it doesn't actually impact the way the story unfolds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've implemented your suggestions on clarifying the SCP-3082-2 designation in the 'Locations' tab and on ending the Lucy/Wexley conversation with a little more finality.

I agree with you that the second Aria interview is the least interesting of them all — however, I thought and still think that jumping from the first to the last with only a note of the second would have been too big of a leap.

My original idea for this SCP was, as you stated very concisely, "clashing parties in their limbo". However, I realized that exploring that concept throughout the story would have required a resolution in order to be satisfactory, and I didn't want the story to be resolved.1 Since I wasn't willing to tell how the narrative ended, I chose instead to tell how it began. Although I really appreciate where you're coming from about this, I don't want to change the story to pursue a resolution — I'd rather that the body of the article remain what is the "middle" of larger narrative.

It's certainly your right to downvote for your dissatisfaction with the story progression.

Footnotes
1. Mostly because, in my mind, the narrative ends with both the pocket dimension and the Morrison sisters disappearing as the Neverlanders are freed, and then the Neverlanders themselves being treated to high-grade amnestics before getting funneled into the foster care system. It would have been a rather bleak finish to an article whose atmosphere I wanted to be more magical and unconstrained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very immersive and wonderfully structured although I was really disappointed to find that the story did not stay with the main inhabitants.

As a couple minor points for improvement:

I'll note that the initial mention of -2 threw me for a loop, as it was casually mentioned prior to being properly established, and with several designations introduced by that point, I'd thought I missed something. This could be fixed by either shifting the Location and Residents tabs, or the inclusion of a simple "(see 'Residents' tab)" when the designation is brought up.

The conversation between Lucy and Wexley seemed to end rather abruptly for no apparent reason. It didn't feel like a natural place for the conversation to trail off - perhaps a parting comment could be included?

My main issues come with the interviews with -2. While they're fine by themselves, and I do like the clever way they achieve two-way communication, the second interview Aria drags on in an uninteresting manner, as the SCPF personnel fill her in on their purpose. It feels like the overarching story grinds to an unsatisfying halt here. More or less the same amount of information could have been conveyed in a quick recap in the subsequent log, if it was even needed to begin with.

Lastly, as I said initially, I'm disappointed with the focus on Aria. When the set-up seemed to be priming a story about the clashing parties in their limbo, a scenario ripe for an introspective look at the characters, and a chance to show some of them develop or grow, we're instead treated with a rather rote backstory sans any type of story progression.

I feel like her story was the least interesting possibility to pursue here, especially in one giant chunk stuck to the end, when it could have been more or less implied in the background while staying with the Neverlanders.

Was going to downvote, but I'm not entirely sure how I feel about this. I'll have to come back to it on a later date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know exactly what goes behind those redactions, but what's there reveals too much of the narrative too early, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good, although it seems to have too much content before the narrative is revealed, and those terrible [DATA REDACTED] don't help, you could replace them with something that implies reality warping for example, or anything to show what Aria wanted to do after she left the village.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it was a very fun read. The writing is technically of a high quality and the whole piece strikes a perfect balance between scientific/bureaucratic facsimile and narrative focus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this in draft and absolutely love it here. Very well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So glad to see this on the main site.

I like the argument you added between Wexley and Lucy; really gives us a taste of the debate between the Neverlanders and the others. Being a toddler for fifteen years would really suck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely good. Brilliant. In particular, I appreciate how the drip-feed of Morrison info throughout the -01 doc kept me intrigued while you went about explaining everything else; how you convincingly evoked the half-maturity of the anomaly's inhabitants through that well-written dialogue; the emotional punch and deep exploration of the central idea; how, when you needed to justify a plot device, you waited until a little while after introducing it to do so, which avoided anything seeming too contrived; how parsimoniously (in the sense of "efficiently impactful") you've structured and developed each section of writing; and the lovely twist at the end.

You clearly put a lot of thought into this, and it's paid off spectacularly. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very glad you liked it. :) Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Immersively built, some nice broad character-work, nothing is overplayed (except perhaps Wesley, but I can forgive that in context), it gets better as it goes on without ever wearing out its welcome, and the last line clinched my upvote. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks :)

EDIT: Yep that license is fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really? Alright. I'll get to work finding a replacement image.

Edit: Done. Is Pixabay's CC0-based license okay?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent work Riolu, you did a great job melding together multiple plot threads while doing each justice. Between this and the apple pie skip you're really hitting your stride as an author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, I think I'd prefer those images to stay where they are. I appreciate the suggestion, though. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup. :D The drawing's a work in progress at the moment, but I'll get it done.

Edit: Art contest submission now done. It can be viewed on my author page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CC BY-NC licenses aren't complient with the sites license, I'm afraid, so that one will have to be removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yea, it looks neater now. However the images of the pitcher plant and morrison code is still somewhat out of place, as they are mentioned much later. Maybe move them to the top of the interviews where they are mentioned?

Just a suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hehe, well, fair point. In that case, I've changed my mind. I have no other concerns now. XD

So, now I can finally expect an SCP-3082 art contest entry, huh? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, Greeny! :)

While I share your preference for collapsibles over tabview, this article with all its collapsed text open is long enough as it already is, tabview and all. XD Plus, I actually really like how the tabview makes the sections feel more like papers stacked on top of each other.

Glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have been reading this draft for some time and the process it was written is pretty much a placid river. I really liked the idea and the parameters you situated to establishe a stable universe concept. …and a giant tree with a tree-village of children…well that's something we don't see everyday. The only concern I have is for the collapsibles, and I still stand for a much linear display instead of tabviews (as I'm mostly reading scips through mobile).

Overall, well done Rio! Take my upboat here will ya'!

+1 :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good point about the first document. I've changed it so that all the non-higher clearance stuff is no longer inside collapsibles.

Also, "WATERPROOG" is now "WATERPROEF", because I still want a typo there, but you're right that mixing up G and F isn't very reasonable.

Thanks a bunch! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very cool scip and +1 for you. However, I kinda dislike the way Document 3082-01 is formatted, it looks kinda bare when collapsed.

Also,

[DRONE IS WATERPROOG]

I believe this is a typo, as the code for G and F are quite different and thus can't be the Foundation's error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Corrected the XXXX thing — I really need to make a habit of double-checking that before I post.

Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've got SCP-XXXX littering the article but I enjoyed the Swiss Family Robinson/Lost In Space/Little Lamplight/Neverland environment. The bit with the leg was particularly good among the interviews. The world building was fun and always felt like it was serving a larger purpose. Very immersive. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope that this article was an enjoyable read.

Many thanks to darkgreeny, DrChandra, RegularSlambo, taylor_itkin, and Sly161 for their brilliant feedback when this article was a draft/concept.

+ Translations
- Translations
  • SCP-3082 in Japanese, translated by Aone
  • SCP-3082 in Ukrainian, translated by murzei_chaos
  • SCP-3082 in Chinese, translated by M-Chris and achsab
+ Image credits
- Image credits
  1. First image (treehouse) is public domain.
  2. Second image (treehouse cabin), real title Treehouse Point, belongs to Tony Guyton on Flickr under the CC BY 2.0 generic license.
  3. Third image (dead trees) is public domain.
  4. "Foundation guest house" image, real title Tree House at Natore Rajbari, was altered by me and belongs to "একলা ঘাসফড়িং" on Wikimedia Commons under the CC BY-SA 4.0 international license.
  5. "First contact encampment site" image belongs to "GingerKelly" on Pixabay under the Pixabay license, which is CC0 based.
  6. "Rachel" is public domain.
  7. "Ahmed" is public domain.
  8. "Lucy" is public domain.
  9. "Wexley" is public domain.
  10. "Aria" is public domain.
  11. Pitcher plants image is public domain.
  12. "Morrison code" on birch bark image is public domain and altered by me.
  13. Quadcopter drone image, real title DJI Phantom 4 in Flight March 2016, belongs to "Doodybutch" on Wikimedia Commons under the CC BY-SA 4.0 international license.
